aliases: Handmade flat noodle soup with vegetables

english: Handmade flat noodle soup with vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for ban mian; closest defensible match is 'noodle soup dish' (FOODON_03315595). Vegetable ban mian is a handmade flat wheat noodle soup specific to Singapore hawker cuisine.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (wheat noodles, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for ban mian soup as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ban mian or handmade flat wheat noodle soup. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular hawker dish entries.

main_ingredients: leafy green vegetables (e.g., chye sim, spinach)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker serving of vegetable ban mian soup is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of handmade flat wheat noodles with leafy green vegetables in clear anchovy broth.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable ban mian (soup) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetable ban mian (soup), CR ID F05060132. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean vegetable ban mian soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 47 is estimated using the closest analogous food: fresh wheat noodles (Chinese-style, boiled), which have a published GI of approximately 47 (low) per Atkinson et al. 2008 and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 27.65) / 100 ≈ 13.0, based on a 350 g serving providing 27.65 g available carbohydrate. The input record's embedded GI of 15 with confidence 0.54 was rejected as it is unsupported by any approved GI source and is implausibly low for a wheat noodle dish.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 244 mg/100 g; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 854 mg sodium, representing 37–43% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and salt during preparation, avoid consuming the soup broth, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles contribute a moderate glycemic load (GL ≈ 13 per serving) with low dietary fibre (0.6 g/100 g), which may cause post-meal blood glucose elevation at standard serving sizes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion with additional vegetables to improve the fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio, and monitor post-meal blood glucose as refined wheat noodles are a moderate-GI staple.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 244 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low (~0.4 g/100 g) and the dish is not fried, which are favourable factors, but the high sodium load at typical serving sizes is clinically significant for cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions, avoid drinking the broth, and request reduced-sodium preparation to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 1500–2000 mg/day ceiling.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, absence of deep-frying, and presence of leafy green vegetables providing antioxidants are favourable; sodium at 244 mg/100 g may worsen tissue swelling at large serving sizes.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume this dish in moderate portions, requesting reduced soy sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, and opting for more vegetables and less noodle to reduce glycaemic-driven systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Ban Mian (Soup) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of handmade flat wheat noodles (ban mian, literally 'flat noodles') served in a clear, savoury broth made from ikan bilis (dried anchovies) stock, accompanied by leafy green vegetables such as chye sim or spinach. The noodles are hand-pulled or hand-cut to order, giving them a slightly chewy, irregular texture that distinguishes them from machine-made noodles. The broth is light and umami-rich, seasoned with soy sauce and white pepper. This vegetable-only version omits the egg and minced pork commonly found in standard ban mian, making it suitable for vegetarians who accept fish-based stock. Nutritionally, the dish provides 54 kcal per 100 g, with 7.9 g carbohydrate, 2.6 g protein, and 1.3 g fat. Sodium is notable at 244 mg/100 g, and a standard 350 g bowl delivers approximately 854 mg sodium, which is clinically significant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Dietary fibre is low at 0.6 g/100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 47 (low) based on fresh Chinese-style wheat noodles, with a glycemic load of approximately 13 per serving — moderate concern for type 2 diabetes at standard portion sizes. The dish is a staple lunch option in Singapore hawker centres and food courts, commonly consumed by office workers and students. It is low in saturated fat and not deep-fried, making it a relatively heart-friendly choice when sodium is managed.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05060132) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the highest-priority approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source food (fresh Chinese wheat noodles, University of Sydney GI Database) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05060132 as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: the dish is not fried and contains no hydrogenated fats; trans fat is confirmed negligible. 3. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.4 g/100 g based on the fat composition of wheat noodles and vegetable oil used in preparation, consistent with HPB data for similar noodle soup dishes. 4. The input record's embedded GI value of 15 (confidence 0.54, source 'Embedded GI Database') was rejected as it is not from an approved GI source and is implausibly low for a refined wheat noodle dish; replaced with GI 47 estimated from fresh Chinese-style wheat noodles per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. 5. The input record's primary_cuisine field was listed as 'western', which is incorrect; corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese' based on the dish's established hawker cuisine origin. 6. Dietary flags: is_vegetarian set to false because the standard broth uses ikan bilis (anchovy) stock; consumers should verify with the hawker stall if a vegetarian broth is available. 7. Allergens include gluten (wheat noodles) and fish (anchovy stock).
